pattern: A substring is 'self-contained' exactly when expanding its window to absorb every occurrence of every character inside it reaches a fixed point, and those fixed-point windows are always either nested or disjoint - so a simple earliest-finish-time greedy over them is both optimal and length-minimal.
hints: For a substring to legally contain a character, it must contain ALL occurrences of that character - so start from each character's first occurrence and keep growing the window to also cover every other character's occurrences that fall inside, until the window stops changing.
If, while growing a window that starts at position i, you discover a character whose own first occurrence is before i, no valid self-contained substring can start at i at all - discard that attempt.

The surviving candidate windows are always either nested inside each other or completely disjoint (never partially overlapping); sort them by their end position and greedily take each one that starts after the last one you took to get the unique maximum-count, minimum-length answer.

edge_inputs.description: several small self-contained pieces plus a larger merged one
a character repeated across the string forces a merge, leaving distinct pieces around it
a single character is trivially its own substring
every character distinct, each is its own substring
interleaved repeats force the whole string into one substring
one repeated character with nothing else collapses to a single whole-string substring, not one per letter
a small nested interval must be preferred over the larger enclosing one on a length tie-break

stress.description: random strings over a small alphabet near the maximum length, biasing toward heavy nesting and overlap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    alphabet = 'abcde'
    return [''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))]
